title: Where and How the Database is Created

content.problemStatement:
Let’s look at how Django organizes your files, and where the database is actually created and stored.

```
mysite/
├── db.sqlite3 ←This is your actual database file -created after running migrations
├── manage.py
├── mysite/
│   ├── ...
│   ├── settings.py
│   ├── urls.py
│   └── ....
└── library/  ← Your Django app
    ├── ....
    ├── models.py       ← Define your database models here (e.g., Book)
    ├── views.py        ← Use models to fetch data and pass to templates
    ├── urls.py         ← Define URL patterns for this app
    ├── templates/
    │   └── library/
    │       ├── book_list.html  ← Templates display model data
    └── migrations/     ← Auto-generated scripts that tell Django how to create/update tables
        └── __init__.py ← First migration file – like a blueprint for your database tables

```
### What’s Happening Step-by-Step?
- You define models in `models.py` \
Think of this as writing the structure of your table in Python (like Book, Author, etc.).

- You make migrations \
Django reads your models and creates a file in the `migrations/` folder. This file contains SQL-like instructions in Python.

- You apply migrations \
Django executes those instructions and actually creates the tables in your database.

- Your database file appears \
📁 `db.sqlite3` is created in the root of your project. It stores all your tables and data.
